title: What is a struct
instruction:
Imagine a contact card. One card holds a person's name, their phone number, their email — all related, all together.

A struct is exactly that. It is a custom type that groups related fields under one name.

Run this. A `Person` struct is defined with two fields: `Name` and `Age`. Then we create one and print it.
successMessage: One variable, two pieces of information. That is a struct. Notice Go prints it as {Alex 25} — all fields together in curly braces.

title: Define a struct
instruction:
The syntax for defining a struct is:

```
type TypeName struct {
    FieldName FieldType
    FieldName FieldType
}
```

Field names start with a capital letter (so they are exported and accessible everywhere).

Define a `Car` struct with three fields: `Make` (string), `Model` (string), and `Year` (int). Then create one and print it.
hint: type Car struct { Make string; Model string; Year int }  — then c := Car{Make: "Toyota", Model: "Camry", Year: 2020} and fmt.Println(c)

codeChecks.message: Define a struct called Car: type Car struct { ... }
Add a Make string field to the struct.
Add a Model string field to the struct.
Add a Year int field to the struct.
Create a Car value: c := Car{Make: ..., Model: ..., Year: ...}
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Your own custom type — defined, created, and printed. Every real Go application is full of types like this.

title: Access fields with dot notation
instruction:
To read a specific field, use a dot: `person.Name`, `person.Age`.

You do not have to print the whole struct at once. Pull out exactly what you need.

Print `Name` and `Age` on separate lines using dot notation.

codeChecks.message: Access the Name field: p.Name
Access the Age field: p.Age
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Dot notation is how you access every field in Go. Clean, readable, no guessing.

title: Update a field
instruction:
Fields work like variables. You can reassign them after creation.

`p.Age = 26` — updates the Age field directly.

Update `p.City` to "Paris" and `p.Age` to 31, then print the whole struct.

codeChecks.message: Update City: p.City = "Paris"
Update Age: p.Age = 31
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Same struct, updated fields. Notice the name stayed as Sam because we only changed Age and City.

title: Zero values in structs
instruction:
When you declare a struct without setting all fields, Go fills the rest with zero values — empty string for strings, 0 for ints, false for bools.

Run this and see what an unfilled struct looks like. You will recognise the zero values from the Variables chapter.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

type Product struct {
	Name     string
	Price    float64
	InStock  bool
	Quantity int
}

func main() {
	var p Product
	fmt.Println(p.Name)
	fmt.Println(p.Price)
	fmt.Println(p.InStock)
	fmt.Println(p.Quantity)
}
successMessage: Empty string, zero float, false bool, zero int. Every field gets its type's zero value. Safe and predictable — Go never leaves memory uninitialised.

title: Struct as a function parameter
instruction:
You can pass a struct into a function the same way you pass any value.

When you do, Go makes a copy. Changing the struct inside the function does not affect the original.

Call `printPerson` with a person of your choice — your name and age.

codeChecks.message: Call printPerson() with a Person value.
Create a Person struct with a Name field.
Replace the TODO comment with your call.

successMessage: Structs flow through functions just like ints and strings. Clean, readable, no globals required.

title: Struct as a return value
instruction:
Functions can return structs too.

This is how Go constructors work — a function that builds and returns a struct for you:

`func newPerson(name string, age int) Person { return Person{Name: name, Age: age} }`

Add a `newProduct` function that takes a name and price and returns a `Product`. Call it and print the result.
hint: func newProduct(name string, price float64) Product { return Product{Name: name, Price: price} }

codeChecks.message: Define a newProduct function that returns a Product.
Return a Product struct from newProduct.
Replace the TODO comment with your function definition.

successMessage: Constructor pattern. One function, clean creation. This pattern is used everywhere in Go — especially in larger codebases.

title: Pointer to a struct
instruction:
When you pass a struct normally, Go copies it. Changes inside the function are lost.

Use a pointer to pass the original — not a copy:

`func birthday(p *Person) { p.Age++ }`

Create a Person, call `birthday`, and see that Age actually changed.

codeChecks.message: Create a pointer to a Person: p := &Person{...}
Call birthday(p) with the pointer.
Print p.Age after calling birthday.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: The age went up because birthday received a pointer — not a copy. The & creates a pointer, the * in the parameter says 'this is a pointer, modify the original'.

title: Nested structs
instruction:
A struct can contain another struct as a field.

This is how you model real-world relationships: a `Person` has an `Address`. An `Order` has a `Customer` and a list of `Items`.

Run this. Then update `p.Address.City` to "New York" and print the full address.

codeChecks.message: Update p.Address.City to "New York"
Print the full p.Address.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Nested fields accessed with dot-dot notation. Real data is rarely flat — nested structs let you model it naturally.

title: Anonymous struct
successMessage: No type declaration needed. Anonymous structs are great for one-off groupings — test data, config values, small response shapes. No name, no fuss.

title: Slice of structs
instruction:
In real programs you rarely have just one struct. You have a list of them — a slice of structs.

`people := []Person{{...}, {...}, {...}}`

Loop through the slice and print each person's name and age.
hint: for _, p := range people { fmt.Printf("%s is %d\n", p.Name, p.Age) }

codeChecks.message: Loop through people with range: for _, p := range people
Access p.Name inside the loop.
Access p.Age inside the loop.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Slice of structs — the backbone of almost every Go application. User lists, product catalogs, API responses. All of these are slices of structs.

title: Methods
instruction:
A method is a function attached to a struct. Instead of `printPerson(p)` you write `p.Print()`.

The receiver `(p Person)` before the function name links the method to the type:

```
func (p Person) Greet() {
    fmt.Println("Hello, I'm", p.Name)
}
```

Add a `Summary()` method to `Product` that prints `"Name: X, Price: $Y.YY"`. Then call it.
hint: func (p Product) Summary() { fmt.Printf("Name: %s, Price: $%.2f\n", p.Name, p.Price) }

codeChecks.message: Add a method: func (p Product) Summary() { ... }
Call p.Summary() in main.
Replace the TODO comment with your method definition.

successMessage: Methods belong to a type. p.Summary() is cleaner than printProduct(p). And it scales — add a dozen methods, everything stays organised.

title: Pointer receiver methods
instruction:
A method with a pointer receiver can modify the struct it is attached to.

`func (p *Person) HaveBirthday() { p.Age++ }`

The `*` makes it a pointer receiver — changes to `p` inside the method affect the original.

Call `HaveBirthday` and print the age before and after.

codeChecks.message: Call p.HaveBirthday() on the struct.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 24 before, 25 after. The pointer receiver method mutated the real struct, not a copy. Use pointer receivers whenever a method needs to change the struct.

title: Struct tags
instruction:
Struct tags are metadata attached to fields. They tell encoding libraries how to handle each field.

The most common use is JSON encoding:

```
type User struct {
    Name  string `json:"name"`
    Email string `json:"email"`
}
```

The tag `json:"name"` tells Go's JSON encoder to use `name` (lowercase) in the JSON output instead of `Name`.

Run this and see the JSON output.
successMessage: The Go struct with capital letters became lowercase JSON keys. Struct tags control serialization — JSON, XML, database column names, form parsing. You'll use them in every real API.

title: Build a contact book
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a contact book from scratch:

1. Define a `Contact` struct with fields: `Name` (string), `Phone` (string), `Email` (string), `City` (string)
2. Add a `Print()` method that prints all four fields formatted
3. Create a `[]Contact` slice with at least 3 contacts
4. Loop through and call `.Print()` on each one

Output format for each contact:
```
Name: Alex | Phone: 555-0101 | Email: alex@email.com | City: NYC
```
hint: func (c Contact) Print() { fmt.Printf("Name: %s | Phone: %s | Email: %s | City: %s\n", c.Name, c.Phone, c.Email, c.City) }

codeChecks.message: Define a Contact struct.
Add a Name string field to Contact.
Add a Phone string field to Contact.
Add an Email string field to Contact.
Add a City string field to Contact.
Add a Print() method to Contact.
Create a slice of Contacts: contacts := []Contact{...}
Loop through contacts with range.
Call .Print() on each contact.

successMessage: A complete contact book. Custom type, method, slice, loop — all the pieces working together. This is how Go programs are built in the real world. You are ready.
